- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

================================================================================
ISSUE 136 | MEDIUM | docs-code-drift
TITLE: Data-sovereignty over-claim: the optional high-stakes reasoning tier sends prompts off-box, yet docs guarantee "nothing leaves the machine" unconditionally
FILE: docs/decisions/ADR-0003-local-first-data-sovereign.md:14-23; README.md:71; docs/CHARTER.md:33; docs/GOVERNANCE.md:124-127; docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:19,62-63
LINE: ADR-0003:16-17
--- DESC ---
Multiple normative docs state an absolute data-residency guarantee: README L71 "Everything runs on-box (local-first, data-sovereign); nothing leaves the machine."; CHARTER §2.5 "No data leaves the machine."; GOVERNANCE §5 "No data, embeddings, traces, or results leave the machine."; and ADR-0003 L16-17 "The high-stakes reasoning tier uses a fixed-cost subscription path (no metered API)... No data, embeddings, traces, or results leave the machine." But the code's high-stakes tier provably sends content off-box. src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:361-439 (_high_stakes_complete) imports claude_agent_sdk and runs `query(prompt=prompt, ...)` through the `claude` CLI subprocess, which calls Anthropic's frontier model over the network. The docstring (seam.py:9-12) itself says HIGH_STAKES = "frontier model via a fixed-cost subscription path (the claude CLI / Agent SDK using an OAuth token)." The docs conflate two distinct concerns: "no metered API" (a billing model) is NOT the same as "on-box" (data residency). Sending a prompt to a frontier model is inherently off-box. scrubbed_env() (seam.py:160-174) scrubs metered credentials and proxies, but the OAuth subscription call still reaches Anthropic. ADR-0003 L23 "runnable fully offline; nothing depends on a remote service to advance" is true only for the bulk/local tier and the methodology core, not the high-stakes tier. A reviewer or operator relying on the unconditional guarantee for sensitive/licensed data would be misled: if a study's adapter callable invokes the HIGH_STAKES tier, prompt content (derived from the data) leaves the box.
--- FIX ---
Qualify the absolute guarantee everywhere it appears. State that the on-box guarantee covers all engine computation, storage, tracing, and experiment tracking AND the BULK/local reasoning tier; and that the optional HIGH_STAKES frontier tier sends prompt content to Anthropic over the network (fixed-cost subscription, not metered, but off-box). Recommend wording like: "All engine computation, storage, observability, and the local (bulk) reasoning tier run on-box. The optional high-stakes tier routes prompts to a frontier model via the subscription CLI — content for that tier leaves the box; a study handling data that must never leave should use only the bulk tier or UnconfiguredReasoningSeam." Update ADR-0003 to separate the billing claim (no metered API) from the residency claim.

TITLE: ARCHITECTURE.md and package docstring claim the engine ships embedding/clustering/graph/topology builders; it ships only similarity/distance + descriptive stats (the modules' own docstrings say so)
FILE: docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:22; src/assay_engine/__init__.py:14-15
LINE: ARCHITECTURE.md:22
--- DESC ---
ARCHITECTURE §1 "Baseline toolkit" row claims the engine provides "Generic, dataset-agnostic builders: embeddings, similarity/distance, graph/topology, clustering, descriptive statistics. The raw material for any baseline." The public package docstring (src/assay_engine/__init__.py:14-15) repeats it: "baseline — dataset-agnostic baseline builders (embeddings, similarity, graph, clustering, descriptive stats)." But the engine ships NO embedding, clustering, graph, or topology builders. baseline/primitives.py provides only cosine/euclidean similarity-distance and descriptive_stats; baseline/determinism.py provides the determinism harness. The modules' OWN docstrings explicitly contradict the ARCHITECTURE/__init__ claim: primitives.py:8-12 "Studies needing performance or richer ML (embedding models, clustering, graph/topology) bring their own builders... the engine deliberately does not prescribe those choices (ADR-0002)."; toolkit.py:4-7 "the choice-bearing builder that assembles them — which embedding model, which clustering, which graph — encodes dataset decisions and is supplied by a study's adapter." grep confirms no kmeans/cluster/networkx/embed/graph builder anywhere in src/. So the engine-component description over-states what is shipped, and the package's public docstring is internally self-contradictory with its own submodule docstrings.
--- FIX ---
Correct ARCHITECTURE §1 and __init__.py:14-15 to describe what the engine actually ships, e.g. "Baseline toolkit: dataset-agnostic primitives (similarity/distance, descriptive statistics) and a determinism/reproducibility harness — the raw material; the choice-bearing builders (embeddings, clustering, graph/topology) are supplied per-study by the adapter's BaselineBuilder (ADR-0002)."

ISSUE 152 | LOW | docs-code-drift
TITLE: METHODOLOGY §7 and GOVERNANCE §2 body text claim pre-registration is "RFC-3161 timestamped"; the engine ships only an HMAC authority (RFC-3161 is pluggable, not shipped/default)
FILE: docs/METHODOLOGY.md:130-132; docs/GOVERNANCE.md:55-62
LINE: METHODOLOGY.md:131
--- DESC ---
Normative body text presents RFC-3161 timestamping as the mechanism actually in force. METHODOLOGY §7 L130-132: "Pre-registration: hypotheses (data-surfaced or claim-derived) are locked and RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation." GOVERNANCE §2 L55-62 step 3: hypotheses are "RFC-3161 timestamped against a trusted timestamp authority." But the engine ships exactly one timestamp authority — LocalHmacAuthority (on-box HMAC) — and no RFC-3161 TSA client (grep of src/ finds RFC-3161 only in comments/docstrings as a pluggable option; preregistration.py:130 LocalHmacAuthority is the only concrete authority). The runnable example (examples/minimal_study.py:38) and all defaults use LocalHmacAuthority. GOVERNANCE §2's own implementation-status note (L64-81) and ADR-0009 correctly hedge this ("ships one real, data-sovereign authority LocalHmacAuthority... and the Protocol so a study can plug an RFC-3161 TSA"), but the §7/§2 body claims read as if RFC-3161 (third-party non-repudiation) is what happens. HMAC is local tamper-evidence relative to an on-box secret — explicitly NOT the third-party time attestation RFC-3161 provides. The mismatch matters because RFC-3161 is exactly the non-repudiation property a hostile-reviewer audience would key on.
--- FIX ---
In the METHODOLOGY §7 bullet and GOVERNANCE §2 step 3, change the flat "RFC-3161 timestamped" to the shipped default with the pluggable option, e.g. "locked and cryptographically timestamped before confirmation (on-box HMAC by default — local tamper-evidence; an RFC-3161 TSA is pluggable for third-party non-repudiation)." Keep the body text consistent with the existing honest-scope notes.
